title: Dyeing with dyer's broom
subtitle: A reliable clear yellow from Genista tinctoria on alum-mordanted wool
excerpt: Dyer's broom (Genista tinctoria) is one of the classic European yellow dye plants, producing a warm, lasting yellow on alum-mordanted wool. The flowering tops are harvested in summer and used fresh or dried. An iron modifier shifts the colour toward soft olive-green.
sourceNotes: Dyer's broom (Genista tinctoria) documented in Hummel, The Dyeing of Textile Fabrics (1885, public domain, Internet Archive); Pellew, Dyes and Dyeing (1913, public domain, Internet Archive). Luteolin-glycoside chemistry gives a similar hue to weld, though slightly less fast. Iron after-bath modifier producing olive-green documented in traditional British dyer manuals.

glossaryTerms.definition: A metallic salt that bonds to both the wool fibre and the dye molecule, forming a bridge that fixes the colour. Alum (potassium aluminium sulphate) is the standard mordant for most plant dyes.
A substance added after the main dye bath to shift the colour result. Iron modifier (iron sulphate solution) saddens and greens colours; copper modifier warms and deepens them.
The resistance of a dyed colour to fading under light exposure. Dyer's broom is rated moderately lightfast on alum-mordanted wool, similar to weld.

content.text: Dyer's broom is a low-growing shrub native to Britain and mainland Europe. The flowering tops and leaves contain luteolin glycosides, the same flavonoid group responsible for weld's yellow, producing a warm yellow on
is moderate; store finished yarn away from direct light to preserve the colour.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Work outdoors or with an extractor running. Use dedicated dye-pots. Wear gloves and apron. Alum is low-hazard; keep away from food and children.

items.name: Dyer's broom flowering tops (fresh or dried)
Iron sulphate solution (optional, for olive modifier)

content.text: Dissolve 15 g of alum in a cup of hot water and add to the dye-pot with enough cold water to cover the fibre loosely. Add the pre-wetted wool and raise the temperature to 85°C over 30 minutes. Hold at 85°C for 45 minutes. Remove the wool and allow it to cool before proceeding to the dye bath.

If using fresh broom tops, pack them loosely into the clean dye-pot with enough cold water to cover. Bring to 80°C and simmer for 45 minutes. Strain out the plant material and discard it. Return the dye liquor to the pot.

Add the mordanted, still-damp wool to the strained dye bath. Raise the temperature slowly to 80°C over 20 minutes, stirring gently. Hold at 75 to 80°C for 45 minutes. Remove the wool and rinse in water of the same temperature, then gradually cooler water.

shifts the yellow toward a soft, muted olive-green. Add 2 g of iron sulphate dissolved in 200 ml of water to a small amount of dye bath in a separate vessel, enter a sample skein, and hold at 60°C for 15 minutes. Rinse well immediately; leaving wool in an iron bath too long causes fibre degradation.

The alum mordant bath and the plant dye bath can go down a foul drain or onto compost in small home quantities. If you used the iron modifier bath, dispose of it via the foul drain only, never a storm drain, a watercourse, or a soakaway.

items.problem: The yellow is pale and washes out quickly
items.cause: Under-mordanting, or the dye bath temperature was too low
items.fix: Ensure the alum mordant is applied at 85°C for a full 45 minutes before dyeing. Hold the dye bath at a minimum of 75°C for the full dye time.

items.problem: The iron modifier has given an uneven, patchy olive colour
items.cause: The wool was not moved during the modifier bath, or the iron was not fully dissolved
items.fix: Stir the modifier bath frequently and ensure the iron sulphate is fully dissolved before adding the fibre. An uneven modifier result can sometimes be improved by re-entering the dye bath at 80°C for 15 minutes before re-applying the modifier.
